Article content: {"preview_text":"MIT and Stanford researchers found that AI agents consume up to 1000× more tokens than traditional AI workloads.
 
In 5 minutes, you'll understand why your AI inference bill keeps going up even as","media_entities":["3_2059283412104667137","3_2059283906604806144","3_2059284597658337280"],"cover_media":"3_2059294825791496193","title":"Why AI Inference Costs Keep Rising Despite Cheaper Tokens","plain_text":"MIT and Stanford researchers found that AI agents consume up to 1000× more tokens than traditional AI workloads.
 
In 5 minutes, you'll understand why your AI inference bill keeps going up even as token prices fall  and what a new  paper says is actually driving the cost.
 
AI is getting cheaper per token and more expensive in practice.
Those two things are both true. And the gap between them is where most AI products break.
The industry has spent two years celebrating falling token prices as proof that AI is becoming affordable. It's the wrong metric. Token price is an input cost.
What actually matters is cost per outcome, the total spend to deliver one unit of value to a user.
Almost nobody tracks that number. And that's exactly why bills keep rising even as prices fall.
 
This paper out of MIT, Stanford, Google Deepmind, and Microsoft just put hard numbers on exactly why.
It's the first systematic study of token consumption in agentic coding tasks and every finding in it should change how you think about your inference budget.
 
Agents Aren't Just More Expensive. They're a Different Category.
The paper ran eight frontier LLMs on SWE-bench Verified and measured token consumption across three task types: agentic coding, code reasoning, and code chat.
Agentic tasks consumed 1000× more tokens than the other two.
Not 2×. Not 10×. One thousand times. That's not a cost increase, it's a category shift. The mental model most teams use to budget inference was built around chat and reasoning workloads. Agents invalidate that model entirely.
And the driver isn't what most people assume. It's not output tokens, the code the agent writes. It's input tokens.
The context the agent has to read on every step: the repo state, the error logs, the tool outputs, the conversation history accumulating across dozens of turns. Every loop iteration re-ingests most of what came before.
Your agent isn't expensive because it writes a lot. It's expensive because it reads a lot, repeatedly.
 
More Tokens Don't Mean Better Results
This is the finding that should alarm every team running agents in production.
The paper found that token usage and accuracy are not correlated the way you'd expect.
Accuracy peaks at intermediate token costs and then saturates or drop, as costs increase further. Spending more doesn't reliably get you better outcomes. It just costs more.
The same task run multiple times on the same model produced token counts that varied by up to 30×. Identical task, identical model, 30× cost variance. That's not a rounding error. That's a fundamental unpredictability built into how agents execute.
This has a direct implication for anyone building cost models around agentic products: you cannot assume that your average token count from testing reflects what production will look like. The distribution has a very long tail.
 
The Model You Choose Is a Cost Decision, Not Just a Quality Decision
The paper measured token efficiency across eight frontier models on the same tasks. The spread is dramatic.
 
On average, Kimi-K2 and Claude Sonnet 4.5 consumed over 1.5 million more tokens per task than GPT-5. Same tasks. Same benchmark. 1.5 million token difference per task.
At current pricing, that gap compounds fast at volume. A model that scores slightly higher on benchmarks can cost dramatically more to operate at scale. Token efficiency, how much useful work a model does per token spent, is now a first-class selection criterion, not a footnote.
Most teams pick models based on capability benchmarks. The paper argues you need a second benchmark running in parallel: cost per correct outcome.
A model that solves 5% more tasks but uses 40% more tokens to do it may not be the right choice for your production workload.
 
Human Complexity Ratings Don't Predict Token Costs
One of the more surprising findings: task difficulty as rated by human experts only weakly aligns with actual token consumption.
The tasks humans judged as hard weren't necessarily the ones that burned the most tokens. And tasks that seemed straightforward sometimes consumed enormous amounts of compute.
This matters because most cost estimation frameworks and most intuitions engineers have about which features will be expensive are built on human judgments of complexity.
The paper shows that human-perceived complexity and computational effort are measuring different things.
You can't eyeball your feature set and predict where your inference bill is going. You have to measure it.
 
Models Can't Predict Their Own Costs
The final finding is the one with the most immediate operational impact.
The researchers asked models to predict their own token usage before executing tasks. The correlations between predicted and actual token consumption were weak to moderate.
The best result was a correlation of 0.39. Models also systematically underestimated their real costs.
 
This means you cannot ask a model to self-report whether a task will be cheap or expensive and trust the answer. The model genuinely doesn't know. It will tell you a number that sounds plausible and is likely an underestimate.
Any cost-aware routing or budget enforcement system you build has to work from observed data, not model self-assessment.
You need instrumentation, not introspection.
 
What This Means For How You Build
The paper answers three questions: where do agents spend tokens, which models are more efficient, and can agents predict costs.
Inference economics for agentic AI cannot be managed with the same tools and intuitions that work for chat-based products..
The costs are higher, more variable, and less predictable than any pre-deployment estimate will suggest.
The teams that understand this now will build products with cost-aware architecture from the start, token budgets per task, model routing based on efficiency not just capability, hard ceilings on agent retries, and real instrumentation on cost per outcome.
The teams that don't will discover it after the fact, when the bill arrives.
 
The Pricing You're Building On Isn't Real
There's a harder version of this problem underneath all of it.
OpenAI generated ~$3.7B in revenue in 2025. It lost an estimated $5B. That's $1.35 spent for every dollar earned, inference costs are the primary driver.
OpenAI, Google, Anthropic, and Meta are all pricing inference below cost to capture market share. The API price your product is built on is partially subsidized by venture capital and hyperscaler cross-subsidies.
The per-token price you're optimizing against is not the long-run equilibrium.
When the subsidy compresses it will unit economics overnight. Every product built around today's cheapest rate has a hidden liability on its balance sheet.
 
What You Should Consider
Instrument cost per task, not cost per token. The paper shows token counts vary 30× on identical tasks, you need distributions, not averages.
Benchmark models on token efficiency alongside capability. A model that uses 1.5M fewer tokens per task on your workload may outperform a higher-scoring model on the metric that actually hits your P&L.
Don't trust model self-estimates for cost prediction. Build routing and budget controls from observed production data.
Set hard token ceilings on agent runs. The long tail of token variance the paper describes is real. Without a ceiling, your cost anomalies are budget events.
Pull your token logs by feature. You will find at least one feature consuming 10× what you assumed.
 
The Real Answer
Is AI getting cheaper?
Per token, yes. Faster than almost any technology in history.
In practice, no. Not for most teams running agents.
Because consumption is growing faster than prices are falling, the costs are more variable than anyone's models account for, and the models themselves can't tell you what they're about to spend.
The price of a token is dropping. The cost of deploying agents at scale isn't until you stop optimizing for the input and start optimizing for the outcome.
That's the only metric that maps to your business.
